summary: Gruber examines the prospect of custom watch faces for Apple Watch by contrasting Android Wear's open approach with Apple's tightly controlled design philosophy. After browsing FaceRepo's Android Wear offerings, he finds many heavily skeuomorphic and aesthetically dated. He argues Apple will never open watch faces to arbitrary third-party designs, viewing them as fundamental to the device's identity rather than an app-like ecosystem. He draws parallels to Apple's history of limiting customization (no home screen wallpapers until iOS 4, no third-party lock screens eight years into iOS). He predicts Apple will add more built-in faces and possibly partner with brands like Disney, but will treat watch faces as part of the system, not the App Store.
key_points: Apple Watch's 10 built-in faces offer customization within strict bounds — there is no way to create an ugly or un-Apple-Watch-like face, and that's intentional
The open customization approach of Android Wear has produced faces heavy on skeuomorphic textures and 3D effects that feel dated even by Android's own Material Design standards
Apple's OLED display choice drives both aesthetic decisions (black backgrounds) and practical ones (battery life), creating a unified design constraint
Watch faces are more analogous to iOS lock/home screens than to apps — they're part of the system identity, not a third-party opportunity
Apple removed two energy-intensive faces (Timelapse and Photo) before launch, showing battery life trumps personalization in watchOS 1.0
money_quotes: There is no way to set up a watch face that is ugly, or that doesn't look very Apple-Watch-y.
This is what makes Apple so polarizing, and often unpopular with the tinkering crowd — they will limit user configurability, often severely, in the name of design purity and brand consistency.
'This is what we, the designers of this product, want it to look like' vs. 'Go ahead and make it look however you, the user, want it to look'.
Apps are the apps of Apple Watch — that's where there will be thousands of third-party designs. Watch Faces are different. They're more fundamental to the device.
We're eight years into iOS and there still isn't any support for third-party lock or home screens. I expect the same thing for watch faces.
Just about every compromise I've noticed in Apple Watch OS 1.0 is in the service of extending battery life at all costs.
key_insight: Apple treats watch faces as part of the system's identity rather than a customization surface, and will never open them to arbitrary third-party designs — the same philosophy that kept iPhone home screens locked down for years.
